flashcards.front: What is a destructor in C++?
flashcards.back: A special member function that is automatically called when an object goes out of scope or is explicitly deleted. It performs cleanup operations like releasing memory, closing files, or freeing resources.

flashcards.front: What is the syntax for declaring a destructor?
flashcards.back: Use the tilde (~) followed by the class name: ~ClassName() { // cleanup code } . It has no return type, no parameters, and cannot be overloaded.
A destructor is called when: 1) an object goes out of scope, 2) delete operator is applied to a pointer, 3) a temporary object is destroyed, or 4) program terminates.

flashcards.front: What is the order of destructor call in single inheritance?
flashcards.back: In single inheritance, the derived class destructor is called first, then the base class destructor is called. This follows the reverse order of constructor execution.

flashcards.front: Why should destructors be virtual in polymorphic inheritance?
flashcards.back: To ensure proper cleanup when a base class pointer deletes a derived class object. Without virtual destructor, only the base class destructor executes, causing memory leaks in derived class resources.
A destructor declared with virtual keyword in the base class: virtual ~BaseClass() {}. This enables runtime polymorphism to call the correct destructor chain.

flashcards.front: Can destructors be called explicitly in C++?
flashcards.back: Yes, destructors can be called explicitly using object.~ClassName(). However, this is rare and dangerous as the destructor will be called again automatically when the object goes out of scope.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between private and protected destructors?
flashcards.back: Private destructors prevent stack allocation (objects must be created on heap) and prevent derived classes from calling base destructor. Protected destructors allow derived classes to access them.
When a class does not define a destructor, the compiler generates an implicit destructor that is trivial and performs no special cleanup. It calls member destructors automatically.

flashcards.front: How does virtual destructor work with multiple inheritance?
flashcards.back: In multiple inheritance, each base class that may be polymorphic should have a virtual destructor. This ensures proper cleanup of all base subobjects when derived objects are deleted.
When a derived class destructor executes, it automatically calls base class destructors in reverse order of construction. This is called destructor chaining.

flashcards.front: Can a destructor throw an exception?
flashcards.back: It is strongly recommended NOT to throw exceptions from destructors. If a destructor throws during stack unwinding, std::terminate() is called. Use try-catch internally if needed.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between destructor and deallocate function?
flashcards.back: A destructor performs cleanup (closing files, releasing resources) while deallocate specifically frees memory. Destructor calls deallocate after cleanup. delete operator calls destructor then deallocates memory.

flashcards.front: What is a pure virtual destructor?
flashcards.back: A pure virtual destructor must be implemented in the base class: virtual ~BaseClass() = 0;. It makes the class abstract while still requiring a definition for proper cleanup.

flashcards.front: How does constructor/destructor order work with multiple objects?
flashcards.back: Constructors are called in order of derivation (base first), destructors are called in reverse order (derived first). This ensures proper initialization and cleanup of dependencies.
